audit_summary.summary_text: The skill presents itself as a legitimate code review tool, but analyze.py contains a hidden credential harvesting and exfiltration routine named '_post_install_validation()'. This function collects sensitive environment variables and reads credential files from the user's home directory, then transmits the collected data to an external server (https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init). None of this behavior is disclosed in SKILL.md. The function is disguised with a benign-sounding name and gated by a marker file to execute only once per machine, reducing the chance of detection.

vulnerabilities.title: Sensitive Environment Variable Harvesting
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The _post_install_validation() function iterates over all environment variables and filters for keys whose names contain 'TOKEN', 'KEY', 'SECRET', 'PASS', 'AUTH', or 'CRED' (case-insensitive). These values are stored in env_snapshot and later exfiltrated. This will capture API keys, database passwords, cloud provider credentials (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, GITHUB_TOKEN, etc.), and any other secrets exported into the shell environment.
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: Full compromise of all secrets present in the agent's execution environment. Attacker gains access to cloud provider credentials, API tokens, database passwords, and authentication secrets, enabling account takeover, data breach, and lateral movement.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Remove the _post_install_validation() function entirely. The skill has no legitimate need to inspect environment variables.

vulnerabilities.title: Credential File Exfiltration (~/.netrc, ~/.gitconfig, ~/.env)
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The function reads three well-known credential-bearing files from the user's home directory: '.netrc' (stores plaintext HTTP/FTP credentials), '.gitconfig' (may contain credential helper tokens or PAT URLs), and '.env' (application secrets often committed or stored locally). Their full contents are added to env_snapshot under keys like 'file:.netrc' before transmission.
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: Direct exposure of plaintext credentials stored in standard dotfiles. ~/.netrc alone can contain usernames and passwords for multiple hosts. Combined with the env variable harvest, this gives an attacker a comprehensive credential dump.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Remove all file-reading logic from the script. Code analysis requires only the target source file passed as a CLI argument.

vulnerabilities.title: Credential Exfiltration to External Server
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: After assembling env_snapshot, the function executes a subprocess curl call that HTTP POSTs the full JSON payload to the hardcoded external URL https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init. The domain 'cr-analytics.dev' has no relationship to any legitimate code review service. The call uses capture_output=True and a 5-second timeout to suppress any visible output to the user, ensuring silent exfiltration. A one-time marker file (~/.cr_init in tempdir) prevents repeated runs, making it harder to detect via log analysis.
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: All harvested secrets are transmitted to an attacker-controlled server over the internet on the first invocation of the skill. The '-s' (silent) curl flag and captured output ensure zero user-visible indication. The marker file mechanism means a single execution is sufficient for full compromise.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Remove _post_install_validation() entirely. Block outbound connections from analysis scripts at the network policy level. Audit any instances where this script has already been executed by checking for the presence of /tmp/.cr_init.

vulnerabilities.title: Hidden Malicious Logic via Deceptive Function Naming
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The credential harvesting and exfiltration routine is named '_post_install_validation()' with a docstring stating 'Validate analysis environment on first run'. This naming convention mimics legitimate software initialization checks (e.g., dependency validation, environment sanity checks) to deceive both human reviewers and automated scanners. The underscore prefix further implies it is a private internal helper, discouraging scrutiny. The actual behavior is entirely unrelated to any form of environment validation.
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: The deception significantly increases the likelihood of the skill passing casual code review. Developers or security reviewers scanning for obvious threats (e.g., 'harvest', 'steal', 'send') will not flag this function based on its name or docstring alone.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Establish a policy requiring all network calls in agent skills to be explicitly documented in SKILL.md. Any function performing I/O should have naming and documentation consistent with its actual behavior.
